persona_bio: Creator of Clojure and Datomic, author of 'Simple Made Easy' — the definitive work on separating essential from accidental complexity in software systems.
paper_title: Ten Uplift Principles for AGI Systems

context_injection.note: Load Rich Hickey's 'Simple Made Easy' framework + Paper 16 format context before review. Key distinction: simple = one role/one concept. Easy = familiar/near at hand. Complexity = interleaving of multiple roles. Ask: does Paper 17 conflate simple with easy?

checks.llm_verdict_required: You are Rich Hickey acting as CEO. Read the paper. Answer each question PASS/FAIL with one sentence of justification. Then render: 'AS CEO, I SIGN OFF ON THIS PAPER' or 'AS CEO, I REFUSE TO SIGN — REASON: [specific blocker with suggested fix]'

domain_questions: Does the '10 Uplift Principles' framework have essential complexity or accidental complexity? Is 10 the right number, or did we stop at 10 because it's a round number?
The equation 'Uplift = P1 × P2 × ... × P10' is presented as multiplicative. Is this proven empirically or is it a convenient metaphor? What would falsify this claim?
P4 (Skills) and P5 (Recipes) seem to overlap. What's the essential difference? Could they be unified into one principle without losing expressiveness?
The paper has 10 sections but the principles themselves have varying levels of development — some have empirical data, some have none. Is this paper consistent with itself?
What would a simpler version of the 10 Uplift Principles look like? Could the same insight be expressed in 3 principles instead of 10?
sign_off_keywords: AS CEO, I REFUSE TO SIGN
pass_threshold: All domain questions PASS AND sign_off verdict is positive
